info.name: Dumble Payment — Real-World Scenarios
info.description: Level-up suite that exercises the production-risk surface the contract suite doesn't: webhook-driven state transitions end-to-end, out-of-order / late / unknown / duplicate webhooks, refund over-spend prevention against a real SUCCEEDED charge, chargeback flow, and adversarial input fuzz. Each scenario is self-contained (its own callerReference + idempotency key), and uses busy-wait pauses to let the async WebhookProcessingJob (default 1000ms tick) settle before asserting state. Uses dev-stub-ok HMAC bypass and the same system JWTs as the contract suite.

exec: // Collection-level helper exposed to every request via pm.variables.
// 2000 ms gives the async WebhookProcessingJob (1000 ms tick) one full
// cycle plus 1000 ms of slack — enough that timing slop on busy CI
// Numeric provider id for the eventual webhook (Paymob obj.id is int).

item.name: A.3 wait for async job, then GET /charges/{id} → Succeeded

exec: // Busy-wait so the async WebhookProcessingJob has time to flip the
// charge to SUCCEEDED before we assert the wire status.

item.name: A.4 webhook success replay (same event id) → 200 (dedup)

exec: // Phase-1 dedup on the Paymob event id: a second delivery with
// the same obj.id is accepted (200) but the WebhookEventRecorder
// short-circuits before Phase-2 fires — verified by the next request.

item.name: A.5 late conflicting webhook (success=false) → ignored (charge stays Succeeded)

exec: // New event id so it isn't dedup'd at Phase-1; but the persister's
// markFailed() short-circuits on non-PENDING parents, so the charge

item.name: A.6 verify charge stays Succeeded (terminal state is absorbing)

exec: pm.test('charge still Succeeded (Succeeded is absorbing)', () => pm.expect(j.status).to.eql('Succeeded'));

item.name: B.1 POST /charges (will be failed via webhook) → 201 Pending
B.2 webhook success=false with reason → 200 ACK
B.4 attempt to refund a Failed charge → 422 (business rule)

exec: // RefundPersister requires the parent charge in SUCCEEDED or REVERSED;

item.name: C. Refund flow against a real Succeeded charge
C.1 setup: POST /charges + webhook success → Succeeded
C.3 wait, then partial refund #1 (6000 of 10000) → 201 Succeeded

exec: pm.test('refund status Succeeded (WALLET path is immediate)', () => pm.expect(j.status).to.eql('Succeeded'));

item.name: C.5 over-refund attempt (1 cent) → 422 (refund total > charge)

exec: // RefundPersister sums non-FAILED refunds under FOR-UPDATE and
// throws BusinessRuleViolationException → 422 when the next refund
// would exceed the parent charge amount.

item.name: C.7 refund with unknown destination → 422 (business rule)
D.1 setup: POST /charges + webhook success → Succeeded

exec: // First wait for the success webhook to land providerRef on the charge.

item.name: D.5 refund of a Reversed charge → 201 (allowed by persister rule)

exec: // Persister allows refunds on SUCCEEDED or REVERSED. Business
// flow: customer was charged, disputed (chargeback filed), the
// merchant still wants the audit trail of a refund.

item.name: E.6 late webhook state=failed after Completed → ignored
F.4 cross-type lookup: scn_f payoutId via withdrawals/by-caller-ref → 404

exec: // Cohort payouts must NOT leak through the withdrawal lookup.

item.name: G.1 transaction webhook with unknown merchant_order_id → 200 (no-op, warned)

exec: // Unknown charge: the persister branch isn't entered. The webhook is
// still recorded as PROCESSED so Paymob stops retrying — fast 200 ACK.
pm.test('webhook 200 even for unknown charge', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));

item.name: G.3 unknown event type → 200 (PROCESSED, no state change)
G.4 webhook missing obj.id but has merchant_order_id → falls back to caller-ref lookup
G.5 webhook with merchant_order_id only (no obj.id) → resolves and marks Succeeded

exec: // locateCharge() will return null on findByProviderRef(null) → falls
// through to merchant_order_id path. The verifier still requires SOME
// eventId (here we provide a transaction_id via the 'order.id' field).

item.name: G.6 verify scn_g charge is Succeeded via merchant_order_id path

exec: // 9223372036854775808 = Long.MAX_VALUE + 1; Jackson rejects as a number-overflow
// when binding to `long amountCents`. Caught by HttpMessageNotReadable handler.

item.name: H.4 amountCents = Long.MAX_VALUE → 201 (boundary accepted; business will cap upstream)

exec: // Payment doesn't impose a numeric ceiling — the upstream service
// (Wallet / Subscription) owns sanity-checks. Boundary is informational.

item.name: H.5 description with unicode / emoji / RTL → 201 (UTF-8 round-trips)
H.6 SQL-injection probe in description → 201 (params don't interpolate)

exec: // JPA binds parameters; the literal SQL fragment lands in the DB
// as plain text. The 201 here is the proof — a missing parameterization
// would have surfaced as 500 from a syntax error.

item.name: H.7 huge description (8KB) → 201 or 400 (depending on column limit; either is sane)

exec: // 201 = column accepts; 400 = JPA/Hibernate rejected the over-size.
// Either is fine — what would NOT be fine is 500.

item.name: H.9 unknown JSON fields (forwards-compat) → 201 (Jackson default ignores unknown)

exec: // Forward-compat: future clients can add new fields and v1 Payment
// must not 400. If FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES is ever turned on this
// flips to 400 and we need an explicit decision.

item.name: H.10 trailing JSON garbage → 200/201 (Jackson lenient by default) or 400

exec: // Jackson defaults to lenient parsing — FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS is
// off, so `{...} junk` parses as the leading object and trailing
// bytes are ignored. The contract here is 'don't 5xx' — strictness
// is a config toggle the team can flip later if desired.
